mappings.rationaleAI: Historical development of mathematics across early imperial China, the medieval Islamic world, and nineteenth-century Britain, with contributions of Descartes, Euler, Newton, and Hooke, directly covers the general development of mathematics through ancient, medieval, and modern times (A.1). Cross-cultural exchange and the Islamic world address Islamic mathematics and transmission to the West (A.1.a.iii).

Foundational concepts including number theory, geometry from Euclid onward, and the philosophical underpinnings of mathematical truth span the development of mathematics (A.1). Coverage of mathematical proof and abstraction connects to the axiomatic method (B.1) and philosophical aspects of geometry (A.2.d.v).

Historical development of number systems from ancient origins, covering integers, fractions, real numbers, and imaginary numbers, directly addresses numerals and numeral systems (A.2.a), including development of simple grouping systems (A.2.a.i) and positional numeral systems (A.2.a.ii).

The historical transition from computational to structural algebra, covering groups, rings, fields, and the theory of equations, relates to the history of algebra (A.2.e), European developments (A.2.e.iii), and the evolution of the theory of algebraic equations (A.2.e.iv).

Euclidean and non-Euclidean geometries spanning 2,500 years directly address the history of geometry (A.2.d). Coverage of Greek geometry (A.2.d.i), projective geometry (A.2.d.iii), non-Euclidean geometry (A.2.d.iv), and spacetime curvature connects to modern geometric ideas (A.2.d.vi).

History of trigonometry across ancient Greek, medieval Indian, and Islamic traditions addresses ideas from these cultures (A.1.a, A.2.d.i) and contributions from the Orient, India, and the Islamic world to mathematics (A.2.e.ii).

Historical development of probability from Galileo and Pascal through Bayes, Laplace, Poisson, and Markov directly addresses the development of the mathematical theory of probability (A.3.a). Different philosophical approaches relate to alternative views of probability (A.3.a.ii), and the law of large numbers connects to Bernoulli's theorem (A.3.a.iii).

Statistics encompassing probability theory, Bayesian inference, estimation, and experimental design directly addresses the development of mathematical statistics (A.3.b) including stochastic processes and control theory.
Treatment of infinite sets, paradoxes of infinity, and their philosophical implications connects to the genetic method and the concept of cardinal number and the theory of sets (B.2.b).

Godel's incompleteness theorems and Hilbert's programme directly address the crisis in the foundations of mathematics after 1900 (B.3), including the paradoxes (B.3.a) and the Formalist and metamathematical method (B.3.c).

Propositional and predicate logic, validity, axioms, and mathematical proofs connect to the axiomatic method (B.1), and the relationship between logic and mathematical foundations relates to Logicism and Formalism (B.3.c).
Russell's Principia Mathematica and theory of types directly address Logicism and the crisis in the foundations of mathematics (B.3, B.3.c).
The evolution of computing technology from room-sized machines to embedded processors directly addresses the evolution of digital devices from the abacus to modern computers (A.2.c.iii).

History of computing from ancient counting aids through mechanical calculation to electronic computation directly addresses calculatory science (A.2.c), the evolution of analogue devices (A.2.c.ii), and digital devices (A.2.c.iii).
Leibniz's development of calculus and his contributions to mathematics directly address the 17th-century discovery of calculus (A.1.b.i).
Newton's contributions to mathematics including calculus directly address the 17th-century development of calculus by Newton and Leibniz (A.1.b.i).
Historical development of number theory from ancient Greek mathematics through Euclid, Fermat, Euler, and Gauss relates to numeral systems (A.2.a) and Greek and Hellenistic mathematics (A.1.a.ii).
Scientific ideas including mathematical problems in ancient Greece and Rome connect to Greek and Hellenistic mathematics (A.1.a.ii).
Combinatorics with origins dating back three thousand years, covering permutations, combinations, and graph theory, relates to the general historical development of mathematics (A.1).
Fractal geometry as a twentieth-century mathematical revolution pioneered by Mandelbrot relates to 19th and 20th century mathematical developments (A.1.b.iii).
Topology as a foundational branch of modern mathematics, with its historical development through Euler, Cauchy, and Descartes, relates to modern ideas in geometry including topology (A.2.d.vi).

The rigorous foundation of calculus developed by Bolzano, Cauchy, and Weierstrass from Newton and Leibniz's earlier work directly addresses 17th-century calculus development (A.1.b.i) and 18th-century advances in analysis (A.1.b.ii).

The historical development of applied mathematics from Euclid through Descartes to its emergence as a distinct field after World War II relates to the general development of mathematics through ancient, medieval, and modern times (A.1).
Chaos theory as a branch of mathematics examining nonlinear dynamics relates to 19th and 20th century mathematical developments (A.1.b.iii).
Computational theory including the Turing machine model and contributions of Babbage, Lovelace, and Turing relate to the evolution of digital devices and computational aids (A.2.c.iii).
